children.value: . Familiarity with neural networks and gradient-based optimization is assumed.

A central assumption of classical statistical learning is that training data are drawn i.i.d. from a fixed distribution. Real-world systems routinely violate this: a medical diagnosis model must incorporate new disease variants without forgetting old ones; a language model deployed in production should update on new domains without degrading on existing benchmarks; a robot should accumulate motor skills over its lifetime rather than relearning each task from scratch.
) is the study of how to learn from a non-stationary stream of data or tasks while retaining previously acquired knowledge
Task Sequences and the Forgetting Problem

children.label: Task Sequences and the Forgetting Problem

Three standard scenarios differ in what information is available at test time:

children.html: ttt

children.value: is provided at test time, so the model only needs to discriminate within each task.
: the input distribution shifts across tasks but the output space is shared; task identity is not provided.
: new classes are introduced at each task and task identity is unknown at test time — the hardest setting, requiring the model to discriminate across all classes seen so far.

The severity of forgetting depends on
. If tasks use largely disjoint subsets of parameters, interference is small. If the same parameters are critical for multiple tasks, any update for one task can disrupt the others.
Continual learning requires balancing two competing pressures:
: the ability to rapidly acquire new knowledge and adapt to new tasks.
: the ability to retain previously acquired knowledge against interference.

A model that is maximally stable (e.g., frozen weights) cannot learn new tasks. A model that is maximally plastic (e.g., standard SGD) forgets immediately. Effective continual learning algorithms navigate the trade-off between these two extremes.
Regularization methods augment the loss for each new task with a penalty that discourages large changes to parameters that were important for past tasks.
approximates the posterior over parameters after task

Replay methods counteract forgetting by periodically revisiting data from previous tasks, either stored explicitly or regenerated by a model.
The simplest approach maintains a fixed-size

children.html: M\mathcal{M}M

children.value: containing a small number of exemplars from each past task, selected by random subsampling or a principled strategy (e.g., reservoir sampling to maintain a uniform random sample from the full stream). The loss at each step interleaves current-task data with replayed data:

children.value: . This ensures that changes to the weight matrix do not alter the feature representations learned for previous tasks, providing a stronger non-interference guarantee than gradient-level OGD.
separates learning into two phases per task:

This maintains a fixed model size while continually distilling new knowledge into the shared knowledge base, amortizing the cost of growing architectures.

Foundation models trained on massive corpora exhibit a form of implicit continual learning resistance: because their representations are broad and general, fine-tuning on a new task tends to cause less forgetting than training a task-specific model from scratch. Nevertheless, catastrophic forgetting remains a concern when fine-tuning is aggressive.
methods such as LoRA (low-rank adaptation) confine task-specific updates to a small number of parameters, naturally limiting interference. For a weight matrix

children.value: for different tasks can be stored and swapped without interference — recovering task performance by loading the appropriate adapter.
A complementary line of work interprets

— where the model adapts to a new task from a few examples in the prompt, without any weight update — as a form of zero-storage continual learning. The task specification is carried in the context rather than the parameters, sidestepping forgetting entirely.

Continual learning sits at the intersection of optimization, Bayesian inference, and representation learning. The key tension — the stability–plasticity dilemma — manifests differently across the methods reviewed here:

children.html: O(∣θ∣)O(|\theta|)O(∣θ∣)
O(∣θ∣)O(|\theta|)O(∣θ∣)
O(∣θ∣)O(|\theta|)O(∣θ∣)
O(r⋅∣θ∣layer)O(r \cdot |\theta|_\text{layer})O(r⋅∣θ∣layer​)
O(T⋅∣θ∣)O(T \cdot |\theta|)O(T⋅∣θ∣)

children.value: Several open problems remain active areas of research:
: tight bounds on the forgetting rate as a function of task similarity, model capacity, and optimization dynamics are not yet established.
: the field lacks consensus benchmarks, making it difficult to compare methods across papers.
without task identity at test time remains far harder than the other two scenarios, and no method approaches the performance of joint training.

: as large pretrained models become the dominant starting point, the relevant questions shift from “how to avoid forgetting” to “how to efficiently adapt while preserving generality” — a setting where the Bayesian sequential-update perspective may prove especially fruitful.
